text:
Olympic weightlifting is more complex than simply lifting a heavy object and placing it back down. This test of human strength and power has been part of theOlympic Gamessince its inception in Greece in 1896.

It was officially recognised as an Olympic sport in 1920 and has featured in every cycle since. However, as weightlifting is not really a mainstream sport outside of the Games, those who tune in every four years may not fully grasp the intricacies of this iconic competition.

The weightlifting begins on August 7 and goes on until August 11 at the South Paris Arena 6, which is connected to the Paris Expo Centre. Whether you're looking to understand how scoring works or want to impress those around you by correctly identifying and explaining each move,Express Sportprovides a comprehensive guide to the sport.

Discovery+is the new home of the Olympics. The streamer is set to broadcast 3,800 hours of sport live from Paris.

Sports fans can getDiscovery+ for £3.99a month until the end of 2024 via Amazon Prime's video channels. This also comes with anAmazon Prime 30-day free trial.

There are five weight classes for both the men's and women's competitions, with a total of 10 gold medals up for grabs, reportsthe Mirror. The male categories include 61kg, 73kg, 89kg, 102kg and 102kg+ while the female categories are 49kg, 59kg, 71kg, 81kg and 81kg+.

The clean and jerk is perhaps the most recognisable move in weightlifting. It bears similarities to the snatch (more on that later). Competitors must lift the barbell above their heads in two movements instead of one.

Firstly, athletes are required to lift the bar to just below their chin and level with their shoulders. Following this, they must raise the bar above their head, stepping one foot forward and one back, before bringing them both back inside, shoulder width apart, and tightening their body so they can stand still while holding the bar above their head.



The snatch may not be as iconic as the clean and jerk but it is one of the most crucial moves. It involves competitors raising the bar from the ground to over their head in one single motion.

In the inaugural games in 1896, competitors were required to perform a one-handed snatch. However, this was altered to a two-handed version at the Paris 1924 games.

Athletes are permitted up to three attempts at each lift. Their best results in each lift are combined to determine their final score. Once a lift is successful, athletes can progress to a heavier weight.

Each lift is judged by three referees. If a lift is deemed successful, they will turn on a white light. Athletes require two white lights to advance onto a heavier weight.

In terms of weightlifting, the former Soviet Union stands as the most triumphant nation. They have secured a total of 62 medals throughout the history of the games. China has also secured 62 medals, but the former Soviet Union still holds a slight edge with their tally of 39 golds compared to China's current count of 39. The United States rounds off the podium with a total medal haul of 44.

Team GB has clinched eight Olympic weightlifting medals. Emily Campbell made history at the Tokyo games by becoming the first British woman to win a medal in weightlifting. The former shot put and discus athlete transitioned to weightlifting during her university years and went on to secure a silver in the +87 category.

Britain's first ever Olympic champion, Launceston Elliott, triumphed in the one-handed lift and came second in the inaugural games in 1896. Other British victors include Louis Martin, Julian Creus, Jim Halliday and David Mercer.

Campbell is not only set to compete at the Olympics, but she will also be the sole representative for Britain in Paris. She will be vying for victory in the +81kg category in the French capital.

Having already won a silver in the 87+ category in Tokyo, she will undoubtedly be aiming to add a gold to her collection.

title: What do snatch and clean and jerk mean in Olympic weightlifting? | Other | Sport | Express.co.uk
author: Patrick Austen-Hardy
description: Weightlifting is about to begin at the Paris Olympic Games, but what are the key terms associated with the sport?
